mnemonics.title: CAPE Method for Capacity Planning
mnemonics.content: CAPE: Collect metrics, Analyze trends, Plan resources, Evaluate effectiveness - A cyclic approach to capacity management.

mnemonics.title: BASELINE Memory Aid
mnemonics.content: BASELINE: Baseline metrics As a Standard for Identifying Latency Issues and Normal Evaluation - helps remember baseline purpose.

mnemonics.title: Performance Metrics Quick Recall
mnemonics.content: CTOR: CPU, Throughput, Output, Response time - four key metrics to remember for capacity measurement.

acronyms.usage: Measures system throughput for transactional workloads
Contract defining acceptable performance and availability standards
Measurable values demonstrating achievement of business objectives
Defines performance standards for network and system services
Maximum acceptable data loss measured in time
Maximum acceptable time to restore services after outage

visualTips.visualization: A line graph showing utilization over time - peak times appear as spikes, ideal state shows steady 70-80% utilization with room for bursts
An L-shaped curve: flat initially, then rises sharply at saturation point - the 'knee' of the curve indicates optimal capacity
A stacked bar chart showing different workload types (batch, interactive, background) consuming resources in layers
A line with trend line showing historical growth - extrapolate the trend to predict future capacity needs

analogies.analogy: Like a plumber sizing pipes for expected water flow - too small causes backups, too large wastes money
A baseline is like a photograph of a clean room - any future 'mess' (problems) can be compared to the original state
A highway at saturation is like a road at capacity - adding more cars doesn't increase flow, it creates gridlock
Throughput is like an assembly line's production rate - measuring how many units exit per minute
